sentences.text: Can see his face.
He was the director, kind of director there.
I came up to him, not knowing his name and said, could you help me?
I want to go back to my name, but they spoke, Hungarian people spoke all Yiddish because they were more religious people, and no one would understand his Hungarian of the other countries' survivors.
So they spoke Yiddish between themselves.
And I came up and I had said-- I couldn't speak Polish to him, because he couldn't understand-- though I thought I speak Yiddish.
But my Yiddish, so many years in Germany, was not Yiddish, it was German.
I didn't realize how, how I spoke Yiddish.
It was German.
I came up and, and said, I said-- now I know, because later, I learned Yiddish from them-- now I know that I would have to say, [NON-ENGLISH] but I said [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] So I said, [NON-ENGLISH].
I said, again.
He said to another one, the [NON-ENGLISH] to Jews.
I tell you later why.
[NON-ENGLISH] Let her go.
I didn't go.
He wanted to throw me out, and I said, what?
The Germans did it to me.
Now the Jews are doing it to me?
The Germans didn't want me.
Now Jews don't want me?
Why don't you speak Yiddish?
I said, I do speak Yiddish [? to ?] [? you. ?] But it was German.
It was Germanized.
So he said, oh, because it happened before.
After the war, it's very well-known, in Israel that a number of German girls-- who knows who they were-- and even, even some wives of the Gestapo, or they killed themselves in the concentration camp.
But our boys, the Jewish boys, didn't have enough girls, because there were, was a-- a shortage of Jewish women.
They were killed, all, most of them.
So they took, lived with German girls, and went with them to Israel.
In Israel, they left them for better ones.
So they thought that I want an opportunity to go to Germany, or to go to-- I mean to, to United States, or who knows where.
And I stood in a corner, and I didn't move, and I didn't want to go.
And I wanted to be back Jewish.
I said, that's not my name.
I want my name back.
So they talked and talked, and they said, get her out.
[NON-ENGLISH] What does she want here?
Let her get a-- a Jewish boy and go somewhere.
And I didn't move, and started to cry.
I didn't cry before, but here.
And as I started to cry, there was-- I, I owe it to him, and I, I remember his name.
There was a [NON-ENGLISH] from Israel, [?
Smolenski. ?] Look, all the names I remember.
His name was [?
Smolenski. ?] He was from Israel, [NON-ENGLISH].
We have here, [NON-ENGLISH] from Israel, too.
They sent-- they sent out, in Germany, to [NON-ENGLISH] to get Jewish orphans to Israel.
And he was there, and there was sitting, and he said to them, to the ones who want to throw me out, he said, [NON-ENGLISH], maybe she's really Jewish.
Talk to her.
Maybe she's, look, she's crying there.
When I was a child, I told you before that I was in [NON-ENGLISH] in Poland, when I was young, very young, since 6.
I have heard the older people, my sister knew a little bit Hebrew, that they talked among them Hebrew.
You know what?
Anything I hear, I want to learn, and I wanted to learn Hebrew.
And I said, before she left for Israel, I said, what is that?
She said, it's in Hebrew.
And I ask her word-by-word until I acquired a few words.
When they said, one said to the other one, [NON-ENGLISH], it means maybe she really is Jewish.
I heard it.
And really, I don't know, like a miracle, it came back to me.
And I said, [NON-ENGLISH].
When he heard that, so he said, look, a shiksa would learn a little Yiddish from the boys, but not Hebrew.
And they started to question me, and I said I was in [NON-ENGLISH].
And I said why my Yiddish is not Yiddish, because I didn't know very well Yiddish, and now I was in Germany for so many years, and it was Germanized.
So it-- if I don't know-- he said to them, look, if she knows Hebrew, not one non-Jewish girl would learn Hebrew.
She would learn Yiddish rather, but not Hebrew.
They took me in.
Everything was all right.
They changed my name.
Because I had no witnesses.
No one, no one survived.
So, and I was-- then I changed, and left Wanda, because no one else knew me as anything else but Wanda, even here.
Talk to people who knows me.
It's only Wanda.
They don't know-- if someone calls me Ida, or Eda, I don't know who it is.
But because I wanted-- it was a sentiment-- I wanted to go back to my Jewishness.
No one did.
Says their names are [?
Hercheska ?], there are other names here.
Urick, ?] [?
Murick, ?] and-- they're all-- all non-acquired names here, a lot.
But I know it was a sentiment.
I wanted it back, because I know-- and I lost them all, and I want to keep their name.
So I went back to my name, went back to Furth and I worked there as a nurse.
Now it's another story, how I found my child.
You want to know the story, yes?
When I was in Kudowa on Czechoslovakian border with those people sent by the Red Cross, Jewish people, I worked there before I had that fall.
A woman came-- no one knew that I'm Jewish, no one.
They had a such a respect, a respect from Red Cross, no one came near to me.
And I wanted to speak to Jewish people.
They would, they didn't want to come to me.
They were afraid of anyone strange.
A woman came up to me and said, you know-- yes, and if I talked to someone, they said, why I came?
I said, because I had a child, and I'm looking for my child.
That's why I came up from the Red Cross to Poland.
She came-- a woman came up to me, the first woman that came up, really, to talk.
And she said, I've heard rumors here that you had a child in Lwow.
You are from Lwow, yes?
I said, yes, I lived in Lwow, and I had a child, and I was so and so, I said.
And I came up here to find her, back to Poland, and that's why I came.
She said, you know what?
Your child is alive.
And how do you-- an ordinary woman-- how do you know?
She was in my place.
How do you know?
Yes, she was a [?
Vladisava ?] Grabowienska and you called her [?
Junea, ?] [?
Fredricka ?] [? for ?] [?
Junea. ?] I said, yes, her name is the same.
Yes.
She was in your place?
You are here, now where is she?
Oh, you'll have a very hard battle, not easy.

But if you go back to Germany and stay in [NON-ENGLISH], work-- it was an ambulance, not a big hospital, had set up for all survivors there in a little [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Furth was a small city, but that was a part of the city that it-- it was given to us to live there.
There were beautiful homes with gardens, a small enclosure for about, say, 800 people, 1,000 people, small houses, and we had to share.
And there was not a big hospital, a small one set up quickly, with a ambulance, that people-- a few rooms like a hospital.
That's where I worked there.
And not, it was not far from [NON-ENGLISH].
That woman said, if you want to know more about your daughter, where she is, because I can't tell you much.
I know she was alive and she was in my place.
From my place, it took her-- she was very sickly.
She seemed very thin after the war, not eating.
The whole story, you'll hear when you go back where, where you work, I told her, go back there.
I had to go back from there, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] back to Germany from border, Polish, Czechoslovakian and wait until-- inquire and go to [?
Menachem, ?] to the headquarters of the Jewish community, these people who helped me to get back to my name.
And a-- there is a woman from Poland.
She is a rabbi's wife-- the rabbi was killed-- an elderly woman-- she's alive-- and her son.
She has to arrive from Lwow in Nuremberg and she will tell you she knows all about your daughter.
How?
Because she was in her place, too.
She knows all about it, how she got out of that Polish woman, how they rescued her.
The Polish woman didn't want to give her back.
But wait until she come.
You'll receive-- you'll-- I tell them I'm in contact.
You-- you will receive a letter from her to come and see her, and she will live in Nuremberg.
That was not far from my place where I worked in Furth.
All right.
I waited.
About three weeks later came a letter, came to see-- come to see me in my place.
All right.
I went to Nuremberg, in Nuremberg it was.
You know where the trials were, all in Nuremberg, the trials of the Nazis?
I was in those trials, too.
I came into her place.
As I opened the door, she was with a survivor that-- his, her daughter got killed.
Her husband was killed.
As a matter of fact, I found out later that her daughter was together with me when I was in school.
I opened the door and she started to scream.
[INAUDIBLE] her, her son was [INAUDIBLE].
Not a young one.
He was in the, he was in [?
Menachem, ?] in the committee, too.
[INAUDIBLE], come quick, and others, she had in-laws there.
They all survived, and they were in Nuremberg in a private house.
Not all were in camps, in DP camps.
Some were privately, too.
And she called out, [INAUDIBLE] come here.
[NON-ENGLISH]
Pardon?
Yes.
Come, come, come, she said in Yiddish, and see God's [NON-ENGLISH] [? wonder ?] [NON-ENGLISH] Everybody started to run, and they looked at me, and looked at [INAUDIBLE].
I didn't know why.
And they looked at me, and I stood there, and, and didn't understand it.
Look at her and see [?
Junea's ?] face.
My daughter's.
They called her [?
Junea. ?] Now she's Frida here.
Look at her.
One face.
And I still stood there, and they all thought that I'll faint.
I didn't faint.
I thought-- stood there and wanted to hear more.
Your daughter's alive.
I say, I can't believe it.
Yes, I told you.
I told you her name and I told you-- she looks very much-- resembles me very much.
She's all you.
I didn't recognize her because she was undernourished then, and later on, after, you know, she went from place to place, people took her in to, you know, to have a, like a holiday.
Then I said, all right.
I believe you.
Where is she?
And I looked around, and I thought they have-- they brought her in here.
Said, no, you'll have a-- a-- a very hard battle.
What battle is it?
Your husband took her from us.
She was in a orphanage.
And there, I found out, from Krakow about 25 or 30 kilometers, that I was there looking for her, but she was in a orphanage.
And a [?
Mehr, ?] [? now ?] [? is ?] a story, for that story, they wanted to pay me a lot in Israel and I didn't want to sell it.
I said, I don't sell, not my life, and not my daughter's life.
I don't want-- and I needed it, and I didn't want the money.
There came reporters from all the-- they heard the story.
It was in all papers, in German papers, in Polish paper.
Because of Polish papers, I found her later.
I tell you what.
She said, your husband took her from me.
She was in a orphanage.
I said, the orphanage, I have heard here from the other lady in Kudowa.
Yes, he took her from the orphanage.
He came to the orphan-- took her out from the orphanage.
That's not true.
And we don't know now.
He had a [? system, ?] [INAUDIBLE] and United States.
He could go to United States or to-- to America.
Now search for her.
I said, I don't believe it.
I don't feel it.
It's not true.
My husband's not alive.
I knew it before.
I know it.
He's not alive.
He was as an officer in the Russian Army, and there was very-- [?
Katinga, ?] a very know, very well-known place, [?
Katinga ?] what was the name, that the Germans killed without-- anything Polish, not Jewish.
He was not as a Jew.
He was as a Pole.
They killed them all.
So I didn't believe that, too.
I know that he wanted to jump off a-- a Pole told me, I told you, from a train, and they shot him.
So she said, it doesn't matter, but we know that your husband took her.
I said, describe me her husband-- my husband.
Oh, he was very dark, very thin.
So I said, my husband was very fair.
He was-- he had blue eyes, very fair, not very thin at all.
It's not my husband.
So she said, yes, in such a world, a blond one can get black.
So I said, it's-- it's no time for jokes.
Tell me where she is now.
We told you, in search Israel or United States.
There where she can be, because he took it from that orphanage and left with her.

And we know she was in my place, she was in other places where he sent her around from place to place to get her nourished a little, you know, to-- because even after the war, it wasn't food, straightaway, enough.
So he sent her from home-- she remembers it, she told me.
She remembers when she was in that orphanage-- and it's another story-- that they didn't have trained staff to work in orphanage with little children.

And there was a girl without-- because born just before the war, she didn't attend even school at all, even not primary, and a stupid girl, and she was as the head of that orphanage, a young girl about 18.
And she said-- she took us for-- for a walk out on the street, and because it was after the war that she taught us [NON-ENGLISH] to sing.
It's the Jewish hymn, [NON-ENGLISH], you know?
So she said, and we went out on the street and there was a chair, so I sang, [SINGING] [NON-ENGLISH].
And she came up to me and hit me in my face, a little child, survived.
Because there's-- I said, why did you do that?
You cross yourself?
Because you see, Mommy-- she said later when I found her-- because [?
Chocha-- ?] the Polish woman who kept her was [?
Chocha-- ?] taught me if I see [NON-ENGLISH], God will be angry with me that I don't cross myself.
And she taught us to sing a Jewish song, [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what [NON-ENGLISH].
So I sang, [SINGING] [NON-ENGLISH] to be sure, sure that not one God will hurt me.
So I--I--I sang [NON-ENGLISH] and I crossed myself because there was a church.
So she hit me before-- 'cause I-- I [? felt ?] she hit her so hard that she still had somewhere with her nail, she broke her skin somewhere.
So she had to suffer from Jewish people, too, 'til, she-- she was here already, and she said, Mom, I don't like Jewish people.
Even here, I had to battle to convince her that's not true, what they say all.
Now, what did I say, that I went a bit aside to tell you?
What was it?
Yes, when she said, your husband, you have to search for her.
All right.
I went back to work.
I had nothing to do there.
Went back to work, and I thought, one thing I can do, to put in all papers.
What can I do?
I can't search in Israel.
Who would know there?
Although, there, my brother-in-law-- he's old now or he's not alive anymore-- where can he search there if the-- if there is a child there, or in United States.
I have no one.
I went back and thought, that will be the best.
That helped.
I give an advertisement to the paper.
If he's still in Germany, he still didn't leave, and I worked in Germany, I stayed there, maybe it will help.
And I put in German, in Yiddish, in Polish that I'm searching.
Yes, she told me his name, and I--I-- I'll tell you later.
So I put all, in all papers that I'm searching for a child who was [?
Vladislava ?] Grabowienska during the war, but actually, she's [?
Frederika ?] Mehr.
And I am the mother, and I am Ida Wanda Mehr before Wanda Grabowienska.

And I-- I have been told that the child was somewhere with her father, but I put, you know, [NON-ENGLISH] father in Polish, not the real-- I didn't believe, and I knew it's not the father-- with a father, so-called father.
She's somewhere here in Germany, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and gave my address.
If she's somewhere here with-- with that man, Mehr, please contact me under this name where I worked, under the address, and I gave my address.
She was there.
It was in Kassel.
Kassel is top the-- it was very far from [?
Munchen. ?] How, how far, I don't know exactly.
But you have a whole night to travel to go to Kassel from [?
Munchen, ?] or Nuremberg.
She's very much resembles, not only-- she resembles.
I mean, she had some character, too, and I have it from my mother.
Whenever I find a-- a newspaper, I have to read it, whatever's there.
I find a piece of paper and I have a look, I want to know, before I throw it out.
I never throw out that old papers.
The-- even old papers.
I don't-- I don't throw them out.
I just want to know what is there.
She was there.
It was a camp much bigger than I was in.
Ours was a model camp, clean, you know, and houses there.
They lived in huge houses, people, families together.
That man, how he's-- Mehr, my name, it's the same as I, no relative at all.
Lwow, I was born in Grodek by Lwow.
I was later in Lwow, lived in Lwow, but he was born in Lwow.
His wife-- my daughter was born in '39-- his wife and I, two Mehrs in the same hospital near each other.
One not aware of the other one, that she's Mehr and I'm Mehr, but her name was different, we both bore, bore two children, two little girls.
She had a girl and I had a girl, and both Mehr.
Then the war came, and we'd never knew each other, strangers.
It's a lot in Jewish, you know, that among Jewish people, that we have the same name.
They are not relative, not related at all.
After the war came, he was sent to Russia and so was my husband, but my husband, they took him not far, and straightaway, the Germans got them over and took him away.
And he was deep into Russia and came back from Russia after the war.
Many came back from Russia-- men.
His wife and child were killed, both, by the Germans.
While he was away, they were killed.
I survived in Germany.
My story, I told how I survived, but his wife what got killed, both, and the child.
My child survived-- I told you the story where she survived in Poland because of that Polish woman, and later, Jews helped her.
When I-- he was back, searching for his wife and never found her.
He was a very simple man, and he couldn't believe it, that they killed his wife and his daughter.

When it was known there through the Jewish committee-- and it was quickly set up, a Jewish committee in Poland after the war-- when that woman, whom I have seen, she said, your daughter's alive and so, and so, through the committee, he came back, went to the committee, and said I had a wife and a child here, born so-and-so, but not the first name.
But because he was a very simple man, they said to him, your daughter's alive and she's in that orphanage there, my daughter.
That's a mix-up story.
And he said, I want my child.
They gave him my daughter.
She never knew her father, and so thought that's her father.
So the father found the daughter.
But his wife was killed and his child.
When he asked my daughter, where's your mommy-- his wife-- she said, in Germany.
Because the Polish woman, later, when she got a year later, older, told her, Mommy went to Germany, but we don't know if she's alive or not.
Because I couldn't write to them.
So he was happy that he has his daughter, and he was searching for his wife.
When I gave the advertisement into a few-- in a few papers, she was in Kassel there with him.
And I said she like, like myself, even now, if she find a paper, she has to read it, even an old piece of paper.
What year was this?
She went, already, there, a huge, a dirty place, thousands of people there, and went to school there, started school, a Jewish school again.
Went to school, the tiny little child.
It was raining.
And soft, not paved, the-- the outsides, from house to house, nearly fell in, and saw a little paper of-- on the soft surface there, on the ground.
She picked it up and found my-- my advertisement in Polish.

When I was in-- in Poland when she was still very little and the Polish woman took me in to teach her children Polish because schools, Polish schools during German occupation were closed, and she want me to teach her children in the evening, Polish, and I did.
She was near me, sitting at the beginning, when she got her back from the hospital, you know, was sitting here, and before I fell to prison.
Just the first two days.
And I taught and she listened.
When I left, the children talk among themselves, hers, the woman's children, Polish.
And I don't know where they could-- could get a few books.
Maybe the mother had before, something.
And she-- she told me later, and I looked in, and I could learn by myself, E, U, O, A, like this, and could hear Polish.
She still can speak a little, but not much Polish, very little.
She laughs when I try to talk to her.
And she learned a little Polish.
As she had said, she could hardly read it.
It was my advertisement in Polish she found in Kassel, where she was with him.
But she saw Wanda Grabowienska.
And then she-- she worked out something, that I am alive.
She couldn't know exactly what it is.
She ran with-- didn't go to school, ran back.
She was still undernourished, still sickly, still without hair.
You have seen it.
Not like here, she looks.
That was years later.
Ran back.
They lived, a few families in one house.

She went to a woman there, an old woman who looked after her, because he was there [? as a ?] police, Jewish police in that-- it was a huge place, so they needed police to get order among the people, they used to-- And that place, there were mostly people who'd survived as partisans, and it was the worst kind of people there.
Now, I tell you what I lived-- I-- I got through there, through the--
Yes.
All right.
